title: GAN-Based Super-Resolution (SRGAN)
concept: SRGAN adds an adversarial discriminator that learns to tell real photos from super-resolved ones, pushing the generator to produce sharper, more photorealistic textures than perceptual loss alone can achieve.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why should a medical imaging SR model avoid relying purely on perceptual loss?

options.text: Perceptual loss can hallucinate visually plausible but factually inaccurate detail, which is risky for diagnosis
Perceptual loss cannot process any medical images
Perceptual loss always produces blank images on X-rays

exercises.explain: Yes! Visual plausibility isn't the same as diagnostic accuracy.

cards.title: A forger and a detective
cards.body: Imagine a forger trying to paint a fake masterpiece, while a detective tries to spot the fake. As the detective gets sharper at spotting flaws, the forger is forced to improve their technique. SRGAN pits two networks against each other exactly like this.

cards.title: SRGAN
cards.body: SRGAN adds a discriminator network to the SR pipeline. The generator (the SR network) tries to produce images indistinguishable from real high-res photos; the discriminator tries to tell real from generated. Both improve together through this adversarial competition (callback to the GAN concept).

cards.title: An art forger vs. an art critic
cards.body: The generator is the art forger, trying to paint convincing fake high-res photos. The discriminator is the sharp-eyed art critic, trying to catch every fake. As the critic gets better at spotting flaws, the forger is pushed to add finer, more convincing texture detail.

cards.title: Realistic fabric and skin texture
cards.body: A pure pixel/perceptual-loss SR model might produce smooth, slightly waxy skin or flat fabric texture. SRGAN's adversarial pressure pushes the generator to add fine, believable pores and weave patterns — details the discriminator would otherwise flag as 'fake-looking'.

cards.title: Adversarial training can hallucinate detail
cards.body: A downside: because the discriminator only cares about 'does this look real', SRGAN can invent plausible-looking texture that wasn't actually in the original scene — great for artistic photo enhancement, risky for applications needing factual accuracy (like the medical case from before).

cards.title: SRCNN/Perceptual Loss vs. SRGAN
cards.body: Comparing the two SR training philosophies:

left.text:
Trained against a fixed target image
Tends toward safe, smoother textures
More conservative, less hallucination

right.text:
Trained against a competing discriminator
Pushed toward sharp, realistic texture
Can hallucinate convincing-but-fake detail

exercises.prompt: What two networks compete in SRGAN's adversarial training?

options.text: A generator (SR network) and a discriminator that tries to spot fakes
A classifier and a segmentation network
An encoder and a decoder with no competition
Two identical SR generators trained separately

exercises.explain: Correct! The generator and discriminator compete adversarially.

exercises.prompt: True or false: SRGAN's adversarial training can sometimes invent plausible-looking texture detail that wasn't in the original scene.
exercises.explain: True. Because the discriminator only judges realism, not factual accuracy, SRGAN can hallucinate convincing but fabricated detail.

exercises.sentence: In SRGAN, the ___ network tries to distinguish real high-resolution photos from generated ones.
exercises.explain: The discriminator is trained to spot fake (generated) images from real ones.

exercises.prompt: Why does adversarial pressure from a discriminator push the generator toward finer texture detail than pixel loss alone?

options.text: The discriminator flags overly smooth or blurry outputs as fake, forcing the generator to add convincing fine detail
The discriminator directly edits the generator's pixels
The discriminator only checks image file size

exercises.explain: Correct! The generator must fool an ever-improving critic, pushing texture realism higher.

exercises.prompt: A photo restoration app uses SRGAN to sharpen old family photos for printing. A user complains a restored photo shows facial details that don't match any real photo of their relative. What likely happened?

options.text: SRGAN's adversarial training hallucinated plausible-looking but fabricated fine detail that wasn't actually present in the original blurry photo
The discriminator physically altered the original photo file on disk
SRGAN can only process black-and-white images, causing this error
This is impossible; SRGAN always perfectly preserves every original detail

exercises.explain: Exactly! Adversarial realism can fabricate convincing but inaccurate detail. Next: where super-resolution is actually deployed in the real world.
